content:
Mr. [Patient] is a 63-year-old gentleman with significant cardiac risk factors including hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a 40 pack-year smoking history who presents with several months of exertional chest discomfort. He describes the pain as a dull, substernal pressure that occurs with moderate exertion, particularly when climbing stairs or walking briskly, and resolves within minutes of rest. He denies associated dyspnea, diaphoresis, or radiation to the arm or jaw. His wife notes he has been more fatigued than usual over the past two months. Current medications include lisinopril, atorvastatin, and aspirin. He has no prior history of CAD or MI.

On examination today, vital signs are stable with BP 138/82 and HR 78. Cardiac auscultation reveals regular rate and rhythm with no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lungs are clear bilaterally. Peripheral pulses are intact. A recent EKG shows normal sinus rhythm with nonspecific ST changes in the lateral leads, unchanged from prior. Given his age, risk factor burden, and presentation concerning for possible angina, I believe further cardiac risk stratification is warranted.

I would appreciate your evaluation and assessment regarding the need for stress testing to further characterize his ischemic risk. Please advise on appropriate next steps, including whether pharmacologic or exercise stress testing would be most suitable given his functional capacity.
